planting.note: Plant hardwood cuttings or bare-root plants in early spring before leaf break. Cold stratify seeds for 90–180 days at 34–40°F before sowing if starting from seed.

directions: Plant in a sunny, open location with well-drained soil; Dog Rose tolerates poor, rocky, and chalky conditions and does not require amendment or fertilization. Take hardwood cuttings in late autumn or early winter, root them in sandy compost, and overwinter in a cold frame before transplanting the following spring. Once established, it is highly drought-tolerant and requires little supplemental watering. Prune out oldest canes after flowering to maintain an open framework and encourage vigorous new growth bearing the following season's blooms and hips.

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container-grown stock in early spring into well-drained, preferably alkaline or chalky soil. If raising from seed, cold-stratify for 90–180 days before sowing under cover.

directions: Choose a sunny spot with lean, well-drained soil; Sweet Briar thrives on chalky or limestone ground and the apple scent of its foliage is most pronounced in warm sun after a light rain. Propagate by hardwood cuttings taken in late autumn and rooted in gritty compost over winter. Avoid rich soils or heavy feeding, which promote excessive vegetative growth at the expense of flowers and hips. Thin old woody stems every few years to keep the plant open and productive, and wear heavy gloves — the hooked thorns are notably sharp.

planting.note: Plant in early spring in sandy, freely draining soil; Scotch Rose is especially suited to coastal and exposed hillside sites. Division of established clumps or rooted suckers can be transplanted in early spring.

directions: Plant in poor, sandy, or gravelly soil in a fully exposed, sunny location; Scotch Rose is one of the most wind and cold tolerant of all roses and actually performs poorly in rich or waterlogged ground. It spreads steadily by underground suckers to form a low, dense, weed-suppressing thicket, making it excellent for stabilizing dry banks and coastal dunes. The distinctive dark purple-black hips ripen in late summer and persist into autumn, providing food for birds. Propagate by detaching and replanting rooted suckers in early spring, or take semi-hardwood cuttings in mid-summer.

planting.note: Set out container-grown or bare-root plants in early spring in a sunny, open location with well-drained to dry soil. Native prairie gravel or loam is ideal; avoid clay-heavy or amended soils.

directions: Plant in full sun in dry to average, well-drained soil typical of central US prairies; this species is native to open grasslands and woodland edges and does not tolerate wet feet or heavy shade. Once established, it is highly drought-tolerant and spreads by root sprouts to form a low thicket useful for erosion control and wildlife cover. Deep pink blooms appear in late spring to early summer and are followed by small, globe-shaped red hips that persist into winter, providing important food for birds. Propagate from hardwood cuttings in late autumn or by carefully digging and transplanting rooted root sprouts in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring in sharply drained, rocky or gravelly alkaline soil in full sun. Avoid clay or organically rich soils; this rose is adapted to hot, dry limestone slopes of the American Southwest.

directions: Establish in a hot, sunny spot with extremely well-drained, gritty or rocky soil; Sacramento Rose is native to desert mountain slopes and is uniquely adapted to alkaline, low-nutrient conditions that would stress most cultivated roses. Its unusual gooseberry-like divided leaves and bristly stems make it visually distinctive even when not in bloom. Water sparingly after the first season — established plants are highly drought-tolerant and resent overwatering. Propagate by taking semi-hardwood cuttings in early summer and rooting them in very gritty, low-fertility compost; seeds benefit from a warm stratification period followed by cold stratification before germination.

planting.note: Plant container-grown stock in early spring in moist, humus-rich soil in a partially shaded location. Seeds exhibit double dormancy and require a warm period followed by cold stratification; propagation by cuttings is more reliable.

directions: Plant in partial shade in consistently moist, humus-rich soil; Squashberry is native to the understory of boreal and subarctic forests and performs best where it receives filtered light or morning sun with afternoon shade. Maintain even soil moisture, especially during summer, by applying a generous layer of organic mulch around the root zone. Clusters of translucent orange-red berries ripen in late summer and were historically harvested by indigenous peoples of the north for fresh eating, jam, and pemmican. Propagate by softwood cuttings taken in early summer, treated with rooting hormone and kept in a humid environment; division of established clumps in early spring is also effective.
